HTML5 Essentials.aiRubric: Check logic, syntax, and output for HTML5 Essentials.

lessons.title: HTML Document Structure
lessons.theory:
## The HTML Skeleton Every webpage starts with a standard structure. ```html My Page

Hello World

This is my first page.

``` The `` contains metadata. The `` contains visible content.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Profile Page
1. Create a complete HTML structure
2. Set the title to 'My Profile'
3. Add an h1 with your name, a p with your bio, and an unordered list of 3 skills
lessons.starterCode:
___

___

  • ___
  • ___
  • ___
lessons.solution:
Mabel Chinasa

I am a full-stack developer.

  • Python
  • React
  • FastAPI
lessons.hint: DOCTYPE html, title in head, h1/p/ul in body.
lessons.rubric: Valid HTML5 structure with all required elements.

lessons.title: Links & Images
lessons.theory: ## Hyperlinks and Images ```html Visit Google ``` `target='_blank'` opens in new tab. `alt` is required for accessibility.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Gallery Page
1. Create 3 links to your favorite websites
2. Add an image tag (use any URL)
3. Make the image a clickable link
lessons.solution: Google GitHub
lessons.hint: Wrap inside to make it clickable.
lessons.rubric: 3 links present. Image is clickable. Alt text provided.

lessons.title: Forms & Inputs
lessons.theory:
## HTML Forms
Forms collect user input.

```html
<form action='/submit' method='POST'>
  <label for='name'>Name:</label>
  <input type='text' id='name' name='name' required>
  <input type='email' id='email' name='email'>
  <textarea name='message' rows='4'></textarea>
  <button type='submit'>Send</button>
</form>
```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Registration Form
1. Create a form with fields: Full Name, Email, Password, Age
2. Use appropriate input types (text, email, password, number)
3. Add a submit button
lessons.starterCode:
<form>
  <label>Name:</label>
  <input type='___' name='name' required>
  
  <label>Email:</label>
  <input type='___' name='email' required>
  
  <label>Password:</label>
  <input type='___' name='password' required>
  
  <label>Age:</label>
  <input type='___' name='age' min='1' max='120'>
  
  <button type='___'>Register</button>
</form>
lessons.solution:
<form>
  <label>Name:</label>
  <input type='text' name='name' required>
  
  <label>Email:</label>
  <input type='email' name='email' required>
  
  <label>Password:</label>
  <input type='password' name='password' required>
  
  <label>Age:</label>
  <input type='number' name='age' min='1' max='120'>
  
  <button type='submit'>Register</button>
</form>
lessons.hint: text, email, password, number are the input types. submit for the button.
lessons.rubric: 4 inputs with correct types. Submit button present.

lessons.title: Semantic HTML
lessons.theory:
## Semantic Tags
Semantic tags give meaning to your content.

```html
<header>  — top of page
<nav>     — navigation links
<main>    — main content
<article> — self-contained content
<aside>   — sidebar
<footer>  — bottom of page
<section> — themed grouping
```

They improve SEO and accessibility over generic `<div>` tags.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Blog Layout
1. Create a header with a nav containing 3 links
2. A main with an article (heading + 2 paragraphs)
3. An aside with related links
4. A footer with copyright
lessons.starterCode:
<___>

My Blog

<___> Home About Contact <___> <___>

My First Post

Welcome to my blog!

More content here...

<___>

Related Posts

  • Post 1
  • Post 2
<___>

© 2024 My Blog
lessons.solution:
My Blog

Home About Contact

My First Post

Welcome to my blog!

More content here...

Related Posts

  • Post 1
  • Post 2

© 2024 My Blog
lessons.hint: header > nav. main > article + aside. footer at bottom.
lessons.rubric: All 6 semantic tags used correctly.

lessons.title: Tables
lessons.theory:
## HTML Tables ```html
Name Score
Alice 95
``` `` for headers, `` for data rows.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Student Grades Table
1. Create a table with headers: Name, Math, Science, Average
2. Add 3 student rows with grades
3. Use thead and tbody
lessons.starterCode:
<___> <___>
Name Math Science Average
Alice 90 85 87.5
___ ___ ___ ___
lessons.solution:
Name Math Science Average
Alice 90 85 87.5
Bob 78 82 80.0
lessons.hint: thead wraps header row. tbody wraps data rows.
lessons.rubric: Table has thead/tbody. At least 2 data rows. 4 columns.

CSS Styling.aiRubric: Check logic, syntax, and output for CSS Styling.

lessons.title: Selectors & Properties
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Style a Card
1. Create a `.card` class with padding, background, border-radius, and box-shadow
2. Style the card's h2 with a custom color
3. Style a `.btn` class for a button
lessons.hint: Use rgba for shadow opacity. hex codes for colors.
lessons.rubric: Card has shadow, rounded corners. Button is styled.

lessons.title: Flexbox Layout
lessons.theory:
## Flexbox
Flexbox is the modern way to create layouts.

```css
.container {
  display: flex;
  justify-content: center;  /* horizontal */
  align-items: center;      /* vertical */
  gap: 16px;                /* space between items */
}

.item { flex: 1; }  /* Each item takes equal space */
```

Key properties: `flex-direction`, `justify-content`, `align-items`, `flex-wrap`, `gap`.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Navigation Bar
1. Create a flex container for a navbar
2. Logo on the left, links on the right
3. Center items vertically
4. Add spacing between links
lessons.hint: space-between pushes logo left and links right. center for vertical alignment.
lessons.rubric: Navbar is flex with space-between. Links are flex with gap.

lessons.title: CSS Grid
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Photo Gallery Grid
1. Create a 3-column grid with 20px gap
2. Make it responsive (1 column on mobile)
3. Style each grid item as a card
lessons.hint: repeat(3, 1fr) for 3 equal columns. 1fr for single column on mobile.
lessons.rubric: 3-column grid. Responsive to 1 column. Items styled as cards.

lessons.title: Animations & Transitions
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Animated Button
1. Create a button with a smooth hover transition
2. On hover: scale up slightly, change background color
3. Add a pulse keyframe animation
lessons.hint: 0.3s for transition. scale(1.05) for slight growth. 2s infinite for pulse.
lessons.rubric: Smooth hover transition. Keyframe pulse animation defined and applied.

lessons.title: CSS Variables & Dark Mode
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Theme System
1. Define CSS variables for light mode in `:root`
2. Define dark mode variables in `[data-theme='dark']`
3. Use the variables in body and button styles
lessons.hint: Define with --name: value. Use with var(--name).
lessons.rubric: Variables defined for both themes. Used consistently in styles.

JS Basics.aiRubric: Check logic, syntax, and output for JS Basics.

lessons.title: Variables & Console
lessons.theory:
## JavaScript Variables

```javascript
// Modern variable declarations
const name = 'Alice';  // Cannot be reassigned
let age = 25;          // Can be reassigned
// var is old-style, avoid it

console.log(`Hello, ${name}! Age: ${age}`);

// Data types
const num = 42;         // number
const text = 'hello';   // string
const flag = true;      // boolean
const empty = null;     // null
const arr = [1, 2, 3];  // array
const obj = {x: 1};     // object
```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Variable Practice
1. Declare `name` (const), `age` (let), `isStudent` (const)
2. Log a greeting using template literals
3. Change age to age + 1 and log again
lessons.hint: const for fixed values, let for changeable. Template literals use backticks.
lessons.rubric: Variables declared correctly. Template literals used. Age incremented.

lessons.title: Functions & Arrow Functions
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Calculator Functions
1. Create an arrow function `add(a, b)`
2. Create an arrow function `subtract(a, b)`
3. Create a `calculate(a, b, operation)` that calls the right function
4. Test with a few examples
lessons.hint: Pass the function itself (without parentheses) as an argument.
lessons.rubric: Arrow functions defined. Higher-order function works. Output: 13, 7.

lessons.title: Arrays & Methods
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Student Data Processing
1. Given an array of scores, use `.map()` to add 5 bonus points to each
2. Use `.filter()` to keep only passing scores (>= 60)
3. Use `.reduce()` to find the total of passing scores
lessons.hint: .map() transforms, .filter() keeps matches, .reduce() accumulates with initial value 0.
lessons.rubric: All 3 array methods used correctly. Chain produces correct results.

lessons.title: Objects & Destructuring
lessons.instructions:
## Task: User Profile
1. Create a user object with name, email, and scores array
2. Destructure the properties
3. Use spread to create an updated copy with a new field
4. Print both objects
lessons.hint: Destructure with matching property names. ...spread copies all properties.
lessons.rubric: Object created. Destructured. Spread creates new object with added field.

lessons.title: Promises & Async/Await
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Simulated API Call
1. Create a function `fetchUser(id)` that returns a Promise
2. Resolve with a user object after 1 second
3. Create an async function that awaits and logs the result
lessons.hint: resolve() fulfills the promise. await pauses until promise resolves.
lessons.rubric: Promise created with setTimeout. Async/await used. User object logged.

React Fundamentals.aiRubric: Check logic, syntax, and output for React Fundamentals.

lessons.title: What is React?
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Create a React Component
1. Define a `ProfileCard` functional component
2. It should accept `props.username` and `props.bio`
3. Return a simulated JSX string representing the UI
lessons.hint: Access props properties using props.propertyName.
lessons.rubric: ProfileCard generates correct simulated HTML strings using props.

lessons.title: State (useState)
lessons.instructions:
## Task: State Simulator
1. Create a `useState` mock function that returns a value and a setter
2. Use it to simulate a counter component's logic
3. Call the setter and see the value update
lessons.hint: Set internalState to newValue. Return [internalState, setState] array.
lessons.rubric: State updates to 1. Re-render preserves state value (1, not 0).

Next.js.aiRubric: Check logic, syntax, and output for Next.js.

lessons.title: Intro to Next.js
lessons.theory:
## The React Framework
Next.js is a framework built on top of React. It provides:
- **Server-Side Rendering (SSR)**: HTML is generated on the server (better for SEO)
- **Static Site Generation (SSG)**: HTML is generated at build time (super fast)
- **File-system Routing**: Files in the `pages/` or `app/` directory automatically become routes
- **API Routes**: Build your backend API directly in Next.js
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Routing in Next.js
1. Next.js uses file-based routing. Match the file path to its URL route.
2. Match: `pages/index.js`, `pages/about.js`, `pages/blog/[id].js`
3. URL patterns: `/`, `/about`, `/blog/123`
lessons.hint: index.js maps to the root (/). Brackets [id] mean a dynamic route.
lessons.rubric: Files correctly mapped to their Next.js URLs.

lessons.title: Data Fetching
lessons.theory:
## getServerSideProps vs getStaticProps
Next.js allows you to fetch data before the page loads.

- `getStaticProps`: Fetches data at **build time**. Use for blogs, documentation (doesn't change often).
- `getServerSideProps`: Fetches data on **every request**. Use for user dashboards, real-time data.
- `useEffect` (Client-side): Fetches data in the browser after the page loads.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Choose Data Fetching Method
1. Recommend a fetching method based on the use case
2. Use cases: 'public_blog', 'user_dashboard', 'real_time_chat'
3. Methods: 'getStaticProps', 'getServerSideProps', 'client-side'
lessons.hint: Blog = static. Dashboard = server-side. Chat = client-side.
lessons.rubric: Methods correctly matched to their use cases.
